stem: Let \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = ( ([[a]])y,\; ([[b]])x,\; ([[c]])z ) \). Using Stokes' theorem, evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \), where \( C \) is the boundary of the rectangle \( 0 \le x \le [[W]],\; 0 \le y \le [[H]] \) in the plane \( z = 0 \), oriented counterclockwise when viewed from above.
solution: Stokes' theorem gives \( \oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} = \iint_S (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{k}\,dA \). Here \( (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{k} = \partial_x([[b]]x) - \partial_y([[a]]y) = ([[b]]) - ([[a]]) \), so over the rectangle the value is \( (([[b]])-([[a]]))\cdot[[W]]\cdot[[H]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reverses the curl subtraction, using \( \partial_y P - \partial_x Q \).
This adds the two partials instead of subtracting them.
This gives only the (constant) curl component, forgetting to multiply by the area \( [[W]]\cdot[[H]] \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = ( ([[a]])y,\; ([[b]])x,\; ([[c]])z ) \), and let \( C \) be the circle \( x^{2}+y^{2}=[[R]]^{2} \) in the plane \( z=0 \), oriented counterclockwise when viewed from above. Use Stokes' theorem to evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \).
solution: Stokes' theorem gives \( \oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} = \iint_S (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{k}\,dA \), where \( S \) is the disk \( x^2+y^2\le [[R]]^2 \). Here \( (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{k} = ([[b]]) - ([[a]]) \) is constant, so the integral is this constant times the disk's area \( \pi([[R]])^2 \), giving \( (([[b]])-([[a]]))\cdot\pi([[R]])^2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reverses the curl subtraction, using \( \partial_y P - \partial_x Q \).
This adds the two partials instead of subtracting them.
This uses the circle's circumference \( 2\pi R \) instead of the disk's area \( \pi R^2 \).
This uses \( \pi R \) instead of \( \pi R^2 \) for the disk's area.
This adds an extra factor of \( \pi \) to the correct answer.
This subtracts an extra factor of \( \pi \) from the correct answer.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = ( ([[a]])y,\; ([[b]])x,\; ([[c]])z ) \), and let \( C \) be the boundary of the triangle with vertices \( (0,0,0) \), \( ([[W]],0,0) \), and \( (0,[[H]],0) \), oriented counterclockwise when viewed from above. Use Stokes' theorem to evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \).
solution: Stokes' theorem gives \( \oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} = \iint_S (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{k}\,dA \), where \( S \) is the triangular region with legs \( [[W]] \) and \( [[H]] \) on the axes. Here \( (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{k} = ([[b]]) - ([[a]]) \) is constant, so the integral equals this constant times the triangle's area \( \tfrac12\cdot[[W]]\cdot[[H]] \), giving \( (([[b]])-([[a]]))\cdot\tfrac12\cdot[[W]]\cdot[[H]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reverses the curl subtraction, using \( \partial_y P - \partial_x Q \).
This adds the two partials instead of subtracting them.
This uses the full rectangle's area \( W\cdot H \) instead of the triangle's area \( \tfrac12 W H \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = ( ([[a]])y,\; ([[b]])z,\; ([[c]])x ) \). Let \( S \) be the portion of the (tilted) plane \( z = ([[alpha]])x + ([[beta]])y \) lying above the rectangle \( 0 \le x \le [[W]],\; 0 \le y \le [[H]] \), oriented with upward-pointing normal, and let \( C \) be its boundary, oriented consistently with \( S \)'s orientation. Use Stokes' theorem to evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \).
solution: Here \( \nabla\times\mathbf{F} = (-([[b]]), -([[c]]), -([[a]])) \) (a constant vector). For the graph \( z=g(x,y)=([[alpha]])x+([[beta]])y \) with upward orientation, \( \mathbf{n}\,dS = (-g_x,-g_y,1)\,dA = (-([[alpha]]), -([[beta]]), 1)\,dA \). So \( (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{n}\,dS = \big[(-([[b]]))(-([[alpha]])) + (-([[c]]))(-([[beta]])) + (-([[a]]))(1)\big]\,dA = \big[([[b]])([[alpha]]) + ([[c]])([[beta]]) - ([[a]])\big]\,dA \), a constant. Multiplying by the rectangle's area \( [[W]]\cdot[[H]] \) gives \( \displaystyle\oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses only two of the three curl components (\( \partial_x F_3 \)-style shortcut), as if \( \mathbf{n}=\mathbf{k} \) on this tilted surface.
This mistakenly treats the curl components as \( (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{k} = b-a \), the horizontal-plane formula, ignoring the plane's tilt.
This uses only the \( z \)-component of the curl, \( -a \), forgetting the \( x \)- and \( y \)-components entirely contribute through the tilted normal.
This omits the multiplication by the base rectangle's area \( [[W]]\cdot[[H]] \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
